turns.name: SAMUEL A. ALITO, JR.
turns.text: We are supposed to be neutral court of law and that means that we have an obligation to apply the same legal rules regardless of parties and regardless of the nature of the case. Over the years members of this Court have expressed strong and conflicting views about the constitutionality of laws regulating abortion, but when it comes to ordinary legal rules that apply in all cases, there is no justification for treating abortion cases differently from other cases, but with respect to that is what the Court has done in this case. The Court of Appeals held that the parties that brought this case were barred from doing so by the doctrine of res judicata and the court was correct. Now that holding did not mean that these provisions of the Texas Abortion Statute would escape constitutional review. It meant only that these petitioners, these parties, could not bring another case challenging those provisions in the same way they challenged them in the first case. Other parties could challenge those provisions. Women seeking abortions in Texas could challenge the provisions. Other clinics could challenge the provisions. Other doctors could challenge the provisions, but not these petitioners. Res judicata or to use the more modern terminology claim preclusion is a dry legal doctrine, and I'm not going to go into a lot of details this morning, but I do want to say couple of things. First, although the doctrine is dry and technical, it is important. In simple terms it means this. If a party brings a claim in court, has a fair opportunity to make its case and loses and the judgment becomes final, the party cannot file another case raising the same claim. If we do not have this rule litigation would never end. You know the old saying, if at first you don't succeed, try, try again. Well that would be the rule and that's the rule that the Court basically adopts in this case. If at first you don't succeed, sue, sue again. Petitioners in this case filed an earlier case in which they targeted a provision of the Texas Law requiring a doctor who performs abortions to have admitting privileges at a nearby hospital. They brought what is known as a facial claim and what that means is that they claimed that the law is unconstitutional across the board. Petitioners had a fair chance to litigate that claim. They won in the district court but they lost on Appeal in the Fifth Circuit. They could have asked this Court to review the Fifth Circuit's decision. They decided not to do that and the judgment against them became final. Then they went back and they filed a second lawsuit but they did not try to re-litigate their facial claim. If they had done that they would have been subject to sanctions for abusive litigation. Instead, they raised a different claim, namely that the admitting privileges requirement is unconstitutional as applied in some parts of the state. The district court and then did what petitioners had not dared to do. It revived the facial claim on which it had previously been reversed and it again entered judgment for the petitioners declaring that the requirement is unconstitutional across the board. This was obviously wrong and the Fifth Circuit so held, but the Court now reverses, on what basis? Court says that circumstances changed between the time of the first and second cases. What this really means is simply that the Court thinks that petitioners had better evidence at the time of the second case than they did at the time of the first. This is contrary to a cardinal rule of res judicata. A party that loses the first time does not get a second chance just because that party has better evidence. If that were the rule litigation would never end. A final judgment would not finally resolve the dispute as it is supposed to do, instead, if we just signal to the losing party to start looking for more and better evidence and then sue, sue again. Now Justice Breyer said that the courts holding on res judicata in this case is supported by established legal principles. I invite you to read the Court's opinion and search for those established legal principles, you will not find any. The doctrine of res judicata predates the founding of this country, that's why it's in Latin. The Court does not cite a single decision of this Court that supports its decision. It does not cite a single lower court decision that supports this decision. The established legal authority boils down to a comment in the second restatement of judgment. Now, the Court ignores the fact that if you look at the actual rules in the second restatement of judgment you come to the conclusion that the Court of Appeals was right. But the Court picks out a comment to one provision in this scholarly work and distorts the meaning of that, and that is the basis for its holding with respect to res judicata or of the admitting privileges claim. I will say just a word about the other claim, the Ambulatory Surgical Center's claim. This is not a case in which the two provisions just happen to be two provisions of a lengthy code like two provisions of the Internal Revenue Code. These are two very closely related provisions that are attacked by the petitioners as a package. Their claim is that these two provisions together cause the closure of clinics. They've made no effort to separate the effects of the two provisions. They describe them as delivering a 1-2 punch. Well, if the parties in lower courts apply the Court's res judicata holding here in other cases the law will be revolutionized, but perhaps they will recognize that this is an abortion case and therefore the ordinary rules do not apply. I want to say more about res judicata but I will add a few words about another holding that shows with unmistakable clarity that the Court refuses to apply neutral legal rules. The Texas Statute contains what is known as a severability provision and the issue of severability comes up when we hold that part of a law with numerous provisions is unconstitutional. What happens in that case to the parts that are valid, what matters is the intent of the legislative body that enacted the law. If the legislature thought that all the provisions are so bound up with each other, that if one goes the rest can't function as they should, then the entire law is out. But if the legislature thinks that the unconstitutional provision or provisions can be severed from the rest of the law, there is no ground for throwing out the valid provisions. The Texas Law involved in this case contains the strongest severability provision that this Court has ever seen. It is hard to imagine how a stronger one could possibly be drafted. It says that every single word in the statute and every single application of the statute is severable. The legislature made it perfectly clear that if any part of the law was found unconstitutional, it wanted to save as much of the rest as possible. Court totally disregards the severability provision in this law. The Texas Statute says that abortion clinics have to comply with all the regulations that govern Ambulatory Surgical Centers. These regulations are voluminous. This is a printout of them. The Court holds that every single one of these regulations must go. Now this morning Justice Breyer cited three or four that supposedly impose a great financial burden on abortion clinics and that may or may not be the case, but the Court's holding is not limited to those few provisions. Every single provision is held to be unconstitutional. Many of these are innocuous, here are some examples. Patients must be treated with respect, consideration and dignity. Is that unduly burdensome? Is that unreasonable? Nevertheless it's gone. Patients must not be given misleading advertising regarding the competence and/or the capabilities of the organization that's gone. Centers must maintain fire alarm, an emergency communication systems, that's gone. They must eliminate hazards that might lead to slipping and falling, electrical shocks, burns, poisoning or other trauma, gone. Why? There is verbiage in the Court's opinion, but it comes down to this, and this is what you just heard. It would just be too much work to go through all these regulations and figure out which are okay and which are not, so they all have to go. This is an abusive of our authority. We have no authority to strike down perfectly valid provisions of state law and the work that should have been done is not worked that this Court needs to do going through all of these regulations one by one. It's work that the petitioners should have done in the district court and that the district court should have done. It should have identified the provisions that supposedly imposed the undue burden and the rest what should have been allowed to stand. Because the majority in this case has not done what a court of law must do, apply the law in a neutral manner regardless of the parties or the nature of the case, I respectfully dissent.
